solution: The numerator factors as \( (x-[[r]])(x-[[s]]) \), so for \( x \ne [[r]] \) the quotient equals \( x - [[s]] \). Since this cancellation removes the \( \frac{0}{0} \) singularity, the limit equals the value of \( x - [[s]] \) at \( x=[[r]] \), namely \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the two roots instead of taking their difference in the right order.
This reverses the order of subtraction, giving the negative of the correct value.
This reports the point being approached rather than evaluating the cancelled quotient there.
This reports the OTHER root instead of evaluating \( x-[[s]] \) at \( x=[[r]] \).
This is off by one from the correct limit.

This inverts the ratio \( \frac{a}{b} \).
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This multiplies \( a \) and \( b \) instead of dividing.
This adds \( a \) and \( b \) instead of dividing them.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

solution: Applying L'Hopital's rule twice (or the identity \( 1-\cos\theta = 2\sin^2(\theta/2) \)): \( \dfrac{1-\cos([[a]]x)}{x^2} \to \dfrac{[[a]]^2}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by 2, using \( a^2 \) alone.
This uses \( a/2 \), the coefficient scaling for a first-derivative 0/0 form, not this second-order one.
This doubles the correct value instead of matching the \( \tfrac12 \) factor.
This has the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

solution: By L'Hopital's rule (differentiating numerator and denominator), \( \dfrac{e^{[[a]]x}-1}{[[b]]x} \to \dfrac{[[a]]e^{[[a]]\cdot 0}}{[[b]]} = \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio \( \frac{a}{b} \).
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This multiplies \( a \) and \( b \) instead of dividing.
This subtracts \( b \) from \( a \) instead of dividing.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

solution: Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate \( \sqrt{x+[[a]]} + [[k]] \): \( \dfrac{\sqrt{x+[[a]]} - [[k]]}{x-[[c]]} = \dfrac{x - [[c]]}{(x-[[c]])\left(\sqrt{x+[[a]]}+[[k]]\right)} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{x+[[a]]}+[[k]]} \to \dfrac{1}{2\cdot[[k]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the factor of 2 in the denominator \( 2[[k]] \).
This inverts the correct value.
This halves \( [[k]] \) instead of forming \( 2[[k]] \) in the denominator.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

stem: Evaluate \( \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{[[a]]x - \sin([[a]]x)}{x^{3}} \), a \( \frac{0}{0} \) form that requires L'Hopital's rule more than once.
solution: This \( \frac{0}{0} \) form needs L'Hopital's rule three times (or the Taylor series \( \sin\theta = \theta - \theta^3/6 + \cdots \)): \( [[a]]x - \sin([[a]]x) \approx \dfrac{([[a]]x)^3}{6} \), so the limit is \( \dfrac{[[a]]^3}{6} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by 6, using \( a^3 \) alone.
This divides by 3 instead of 6.
This has the wrong sign as well as an extra factor.
This uses \( a/6 \) instead of \( a^3/6 \), mismatching the power.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
